aliases: Indonesian vegetable salad with peanut sauce

english: Indonesian vegetable salad with peanut sauce

region_of_origin: Java, Indonesia; widely adopted in Malaysia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Pecal/Pecel exists; mapped to closest parent class 'mixed vegetable dish' (FOODON_03311737). Exact Pecal entry absent from FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (Vegetable dishes) used as closest defensible category. No specific Pecal/Pecel entry exists in FoodEx2 as of 2024.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Pecal/Pecel as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Pecal is approximately 200 g, comprising a mix of blanched vegetables dressed with peanut sauce, typically served as a side dish or light meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional composition of pecel — estimated from component ingredients using FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Pecal/Pecel as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted component ingredients (blanched kangkung, bean sprouts, long beans, cabbage, peanut sauce, tofu, tempeh) using FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Tables for Southeast Asian vegetables and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for peanuts, tofu, and tempeh. Input values from enriched_json (confidence 0.9) are consistent with this component-based estimate and have been retained. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 1.5 g/100 g based on peanut and tofu fat profiles.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimated from analogous mixed vegetable dish with peanut sauce — reference: University of Sydney GI Database entries for mixed vegetables and legume-based sauces
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for Pecal/Pecel exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated by analogy with mixed non-starchy vegetable dishes and legume-based peanut sauce components, which consistently show low GI values (GI 20–45) in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 35 × available carbohydrate per serving 22 g) / 100 = 7.7. Available carbohydrate per 200 g serving = (11.0 − 3.0) × 2 = 16.0 g net; GL = (35 × 22.0) / 100 = 7.7 using total carbohydrate 11.0 g/100 g × 2 = 22.0 g per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from peanut sauce dressing
hypertension.guidance: Pecal contains approximately 180 mg sodium per 100 g (360 mg per standard 200 g serving), which is moderate; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt peanut sauce and monitor total daily sodium intake, while benefiting from the dish's high potassium content (250 mg/100 g) which supports blood pressure regulation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high dietary fibre from non-starchy vegetables
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pecal has an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 35 and provides 3 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making it a suitable choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, palm sugar in the peanut sauce contributes approximately 4 g sugar per 100 g and portion size should be monitored to manage total carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Unsaturated fat profile from peanuts and plant-based ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pecal's fat content (8.5 g/100 g) is predominantly unsaturated, derived from peanuts, tofu, and tempeh, with an estimated saturated fat of approximately 1.5 g/100 g, making it a heart-friendly dish; the high fibre, potassium, and antioxidant vitamin content from mixed vegetables further support cardiovascular health when consumed as part of a balanced diet.

peanut_allergy.key_factor: Peanuts are the primary ingredient in the dressing
peanut_allergy.guidance: Pecal is contraindicated for individuals with peanut allergy as the peanut sauce is the defining component of the dish and cannot be omitted.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pecal (also spelled Pecel in Javanese and Indonesian) is a traditional Javanese blanched vegetable salad that has been widely adopted in Malaysian cuisine, particularly in the Malay food tradition. The dish consists of a variety of lightly blanched or steamed vegetables — most commonly water spinach (kangkung), bean sprouts, long beans, and cabbage — dressed with a thick, spicy, and aromatic peanut sauce. The peanut sauce is made from ground fried peanuts, fresh or dried chilies, garlic, kencur (aromatic ginger, Kaempferia galanga), and palm sugar, giving it a distinctive sweet-spicy-savory flavor profile. Pecal is often served with fried tofu, tempeh, and sometimes rice cakes (lontong) or prawn crackers. Nutritionally, Pecal is a moderately energy-dense dish at approximately 140 kcal per 100 g, providing a balanced macronutrient profile: 5.5 g protein, 8.5 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from peanuts), and 11 g carbohydrate with 3 g dietary fibre. It is rich in vitamin A (180 mcg RAE), vitamin K (60 mcg), vitamin C (18 mg), folate (55 mcg), potassium (250 mg), and magnesium (38 mg) from its vegetable base. The dish has an estimated low glycemic index of approximately 35, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking blood sugar control. Its high potassium and moderate sodium content make it broadly suitable for individuals with hypertension when the peanut sauce is not over-salted. Pecal is vegetarian and vegan, halal, and dairy-free, making it accessible across diverse dietary requirements. It is commonly consumed at breakfast, lunch, or dinner and is a staple street food and home-cooked dish across Java and the Malay Peninsula.
common_user_queries: What is the peanut sauce in pecal made of?

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central) contains a direct composite entry for Pecal/Pecel as a prepared dish. Nutrient values are estimated from component ingredients using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, and are consistent with the enriched_json input values (confidence 0.9). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore rated medium.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is set to 0.0: Pecal is a fully plant-based dish (vegetables, peanuts, tofu, tempeh) with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in this formulation. vitamin_d_mcg is set to 0.0: no significant dietary vitamin D sources are present in the plant-based ingredients of Pecal; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for peanuts, tofu, tempeh, and leafy vegetables. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation and moisture data for Pecal as a composite dish are not available in FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as of 2024; cholesterol is expected to be negligible given the plant-based composition but is not confirmed by an approved source entry for this specific dish. 3. Saturated fat estimated at 1.5 g/100 g based on peanut fat profile (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: peanuts, all types, raw, FDC ID 172430) and tofu/tempeh fat profiles; this is an approximation. 4. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database mixed vegetable and legume-based dish entries; no direct Pecal/Pecel GI measurement was found in the database as of 2024. 5. The input source URL (https://www.ifrj.upm.edu.my) is the International Food Research Journal, which is not on the approved source list; it has not been used as primary evidence. All nutrient values are grounded in FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology codes are approximate matches; no exact Pecal/Pecel entry exists in either ontology as of 2024. 7. Pecel Jawa added to Indonesian aliases to capture the common regional qualifier used in Indonesian culinary contexts.
